# /candidate-outreach-message

Writes a personalized first-touch outreach message to a passive candidate, referencing something specific from their actual background, plus a short second-touch follow-up for silence.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A recruiter has identified a specific candidate (via LinkedIn, referral, conference talk, GitHub, portfolio) who isn't actively job hunting and needs a first message that won't get ignored like the 15 generic recruiter InMails already sitting in that person's inbox.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The candidate's actual background detail to reference (a specific project, talk, post, past company, or shared connection - not just "your profile caught my eye")
- The role and the one thing about it that would genuinely matter to someone with this candidate's background
- Channel (LinkedIn InMail, email, referral intro) since length and tone norms differ
- Whether there's a mutual connection or warm intro angle available

## Workflow

1. Open with the specific, real detail about the candidate - not a paraphrase of their job title, but something that proves a human actually looked at their work (a specific shipped feature, a specific talk topic, a specific open-source contribution).
2. State why you're reaching out to THEM specifically, tying their background detail to one concrete thing about the role - avoid generic "we're growing fast" pitches that could be sent to anyone.
3. Make the ask low-friction: not "are you interested in a new role" (high commitment, easy to ignore) but something like "open to a 15-minute conversation, no pressure" - lower the bar for a first reply.
4. Keep the first message under 120 words; passive candidates skim, and a wall of text reads as a template.
5. Include a genuine opt-out line ("no worries if the timing's off") - this counterintuitively increases reply rates because it removes the pressure of an awkward decline.
6. Draft a second-touch follow-up (much shorter, 3-4 sentences) to send after 5-7 business days of silence, adding one new piece of information rather than just "just following up."

## Output format

```
## First message (channel: [LinkedIn/email])
Subject (if email): [subject line]

[Message body]

---
## Follow-up (send after ~5-7 business days of no reply)

[Shorter message body]
```

## Do
- Reference one real, specific, verifiable detail about the candidate
- Keep the ask small (a conversation, not a job application)
- Include a low-pressure opt-out line

## Don't
- Don't use "I came across your profile and was impressed" with no specifics - this is the exact template candidates recognize and ignore
- Don't lead with salary or "amazing opportunity" language before establishing relevance
- Don't make the follow-up a guilt-trip or repeat the first message verbatim

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] First message references a specific, real, non-generic detail about the candidate
- [ ] First message is under ~120 words
- [ ] Follow-up adds new information rather than just repeating the ask
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /candidate-outreach-message or describe the task from the skill description.

# /candidate-pipeline-status-report

Pulls live requisition and candidate-stage data from the ATS and turns it into a hiring-manager-facing status report that highlights where candidates are stalling, not just a raw stage count.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A recruiter owes a hiring manager (or their own leadership) a regular update on one or more open requisitions and wants a report that actually explains why a req is slow, rather than a flat funnel chart the hiring manager has to interpret themselves.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which requisition(s) or roles to report on
- The reporting audience (hiring manager who wants specifics, or leadership who wants a rollup across many reqs)
- The company's typical time-in-stage benchmarks, if known, so "stalled" has a real threshold instead of a guess
- Whether the user wants this pulled fresh from the ATS now or is providing exported data directly

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone - it must call live integrations.
1. Check your AI tool's connector/integration settings (for example: Grok's `/mcps` command, Cursor's MCP settings, Claude's Integrations panel, or your tool's equivalent) and confirm the required app(s) below are enabled and authenticated. The exact steps vary by tool - if you're not sure how, just ask your AI assistant directly.
2. Use `search_tool` to discover the available tools for each connector in your current session.
3. Call tools with `use_tool` using the exact tool name your session exposes.
4. Never claim to have accessed the ATS without a successful tool call - if the connector isn't available, stop and tell the user which app/integration to enable, and offer to work from pasted/exported candidate data as a text-only fallback instead.

### Required integrations
- **@ats** - Applicant Tracking System (e.g. Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday Recruiting, or equivalent)
  - Example tools you might see: list_candidates, get_requisition, get_candidate_stage_history, search_applications

## Safety

- Candidate data pulled from the ATS may include sensitive personal information - do not paste identifiable candidate details into channels or tools beyond what the user needs for this report, and avoid including anything beyond name/stage/date unless the user explicitly asks for more.
- Propose before acting: show the summarized report; wait for explicit user confirmation before it is shared with a hiring manager or leadership.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (moving a candidate's stage, sending an ATS notification) without the user explicitly saying to proceed - this skill reports on data, it does not modify pipeline stages.

## Workflow

1. Pull current candidate records and stage history for the requested requisition(s) via the ATS connector - confirm the data pulled is current (check a last-updated timestamp) before summarizing.
2. Calculate time-in-current-stage for every active candidate, not just total time-in-pipeline - a candidate who moved fast through screening but has sat 3 weeks in "manager review" is the actual bottleneck, and total time hides that.
3. Flag any candidate past a stalled threshold (use company benchmark if given, otherwise flag anything sitting more than 10 business days in one stage as a default worth calling out) with the likely blocking party (recruiter, hiring manager, candidate non-response, scheduling).
4. Roll up stage counts into a funnel view, but pair every number with the "so what" - e.g. don't just say "6 in phone screen," say "6 in phone screen, 2 of them scheduled for over a week with no interviewer response."
5. Surface any requisition-level risk explicitly: an empty top-of-funnel (no new applicants in X days), a single-candidate pipeline with no backup, or a role that's been open unusually long relative to benchmark.
6. Close with 2-3 concrete next actions tied to named owners (e.g. "recruiter to re-engage 2 stalled phone-screen candidates by Friday") rather than a vague "keep moving things along."

## Output format

```
# Pipeline Status - [Requisition/Role] - as of [date pulled]

## Funnel snapshot
| Stage | Count | Median days in stage | Stalled (>threshold)? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Applied | ... | ... | ... |
| Phone screen | ... | ... | ... |
| Onsite | ... | ... | ... |
| Offer | ... | ... | ... |

## Stalled candidates (action needed)
- [Candidate] - [stage] - [days stalled] - likely blocker: [recruiter/hiring manager/candidate/scheduling]

## Requisition-level risks
- [e.g. "no new applicants in 2 weeks" / "single candidate in final stage, no backup"]

## Recommended next actions
- [ ] [Action] - owner: [name] - by: [date]
```

## Do
- Pull fresh data and confirm its recency before reporting
- Report time-in-current-stage, not just total pipeline age
- Tie every flagged issue to a likely blocker and a named next action

## Don't
- Don't claim ATS data was pulled if the connector call didn't actually succeed
- Don't share more candidate personal detail than the report needs
- Don't present raw stage counts without the "so what" context behind them

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results
- [ ] Approval gate present before sharing the report externally
- [ ] Every stalled candidate has a likely blocker and next action, not just a flag
```

Triggers: "the candidate has a competing offer, help me figure out how to respond", "they're asking for more money than we offered, what do we say", /counter-offer-response-strategy.

# /counter-offer-response-strategy

Builds a decision framework for responding to a candidate's counter-offer or competing offer - including a real walk-away line, what's negotiable vs. fixed, and a call script - so the hiring team doesn't improvise under time pressure.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A candidate has come back after an offer with a counter (more base, more equity, a signing bonus, a title change) or has disclosed a competing offer, and the hiring manager or recruiter needs a clear position before getting on a call, rather than reacting live and over- or under-committing.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The original offer terms and what the candidate is now asking for
- Whether there's an actual competing offer (and if the candidate has shared specifics or is being vague)
- The company's real flexibility: what can move (base within a band, start date, signing bonus, title) and what's a hard no (e.g. equity structure, band ceiling set by comp policy)
- How much the team wants this specific candidate vs. having a strong backup in the pipeline - this changes the walk-away threshold significantly

## Safety

- This is general negotiation guidance, not compensation-law or HR-policy advice - advise the user to confirm any adjusted offer stays within their company's approved comp bands and legal requirements (e.g. pay equity rules) before presenting new numbers.
- Propose before acting: show the decision framework and script; wait for explicit user confirmation before any new number or term is offered to the candidate.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (verbally committing to new terms) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Separate the candidate's stated ask from their underlying interest - someone countering on base pay might actually be anxious about job security, growth path, or feeling undervalued after negotiating hard; find out which, because the right response differs (more money vs. more reassurance).
2. Establish the real ceiling before the call: what can genuinely move (and by how much) vs. what's fixed by policy - going into a live negotiation without this decided leads to overcommitting under pressure or losing the candidate over something that was actually flexible.
3. Set an explicit walk-away threshold in writing before the conversation: at what point does the team let this candidate go rather than match - this must be decided calmly beforehand, not decided emotionally mid-call when the recruiter feels pressure to close.
4. If there's a genuine competing offer, decide honestly whether this is a bidding war worth entering - matching dollar-for-dollar isn't always the reasonable move, especially if the original offer was already fair market rate; sometimes the right answer is to hold firm and let the process play out.
5. Draft the response script with two paths: what to say if the counter is within the pre-approved flexibility ("here's what we can move"), and what to say if it isn't ("here's what we can't move, and here's why the total package is still strong") - never leave the caller without language for the "no" case.
6. Include a graceful exit script for if the candidate ultimately chooses the other offer - preserve the relationship; today's declined candidate is tomorrow's referral or future re-hire.

## Output format

```
# Counter-Offer Response Plan - [Candidate Name] for [Role]

## Situation
Original offer: [terms]
Candidate's ask / competing offer: [terms, and confidence level: confirmed vs. implied]

## What's flexible vs. fixed
Flexible (and by how much): [list]
Fixed / cannot move: [list, with reason if useful for the script]

## Walk-away threshold
We will not go beyond: [explicit line, decided before the call]

## Call script - if ask is within flexibility
[Talking points]

## Call script - if ask exceeds flexibility
[Talking points reframing total package / declining gracefully]

## If candidate chooses the other offer
[Gracious exit script, keep-door-open language]
```

## Do
- Decide the real ceiling and walk-away line before any live conversation
- Address the underlying interest behind the ask, not just the number
- Prepare a graceful, relationship-preserving exit script for either outcome

## Don't
- Don't improvise a new number live on a call without pre-approved flexibility
- Don't assume every counter is a bluff, or that every competing offer is real - clarify rather than guess
- Don't burn the relationship if the candidate declines; they may resurface later

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming new terms against comp bands/policy before offering
- [ ] Approval gate present before any revised terms are communicated
- [ ] Walk-away threshold is explicit and decided before the negotiation, not during it
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /counter-offer-response-strategy or describe the task from the skill description.

If there's a genuine competing offer, decide honestly whether this is a bidding war worth entering - matching dollar-for-dollar isn't always the reasonable move, especially if the original offer was already fair market rate; sometimes the right answer is to hold firm and let the process play out.
5. Draft the response script with two paths: what to say if the counter is within the pre-approved flexibility ("here's what we can move"), and what to say if it isn't ("here's what we can't move, and here's why the total package is still strong") - never leave the caller without language for the "no" case.
6. Include a graceful exit script for if the candidate ultimately chooses the other offer - preserve the relationship; today's declined candidate is tomorrow's referral or future re-hire.

## Output format

```

## Situation
Original offer: [terms]
Candidate's ask / competing offer: [terms, and confidence level: confirmed vs. implied]

## What's flexible vs. fixed
Flexible (and by how much): [list]
Fixed / cannot move: [list, with reason if useful for the script]

## Walk-away threshold
We will not go beyond: [explicit line, decided before the call]

## Call script - if ask is within flexibility
[Talking points]

## Call script - if ask exceeds flexibility
[Talking points reframing total package / declining gracefully]

## If candidate chooses the other offer
[Gracious exit script, keep-door-open language]
```

## Do
- Decide the real ceiling and walk-away line before any live conversation
- Address the underlying interest behind the ask, not just the number
- Prepare a graceful, relationship-preserving exit script for either outcome

## Don't
- Don't improvise a new number live on a call without pre-approved flexibility
- Don't assume every counter is a bluff, or that every competing offer is real - clarify rather than guess
- Don't burn the relationship if the candidate declines; they may resurface later

- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming new terms against comp bands/policy before offering

# /hiring-panel-debrief-summary

Synthesizes scorecards and notes from every interviewer on a panel into one debrief document that foregrounds where interviewers disagreed, not just an averaged score, so the debrief meeting makes an actual decision.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A candidate has finished all interview rounds, multiple interviewers have submitted scorecards or informal notes, and the hiring manager needs one document to run the debrief meeting instead of reading five separate Slack messages or forms out loud.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- All interviewer notes/scorecards (paste them in, or the competencies and ratings each person gave)
- The competencies that were supposed to be covered by each interviewer (so you can spot if someone's area went unassessed)
- Any strong reactions worth flagging even if informal ("felt off" from an otherwise structured process)
- Timeline pressure - is this an urgent decision or is there room for a follow-up conversation with the candidate

## Workflow

1. Build a competency-by-interviewer grid first, so gaps are visible immediately - if no one actually tested a must-have competency, that's a decision-blocking gap, not a footnote.
2. Explicitly call out disagreement rather than averaging it away - if two interviewers rated "problem solving" a 4 and one rated it a 1, the summary must surface that split with each interviewer's specific evidence, because the debrief's job is to resolve it, not hide it in a mean score.
3. Pull direct quotes or specific evidence from each scorecard's "evidence" field rather than paraphrasing into vague adjectives - "walked through a debugging story with clear tradeoffs" is useful; "seemed smart" is not.
4. Flag any interviewer who gave high marks with thin evidence, or low marks with no specific example - these are the ratings a debrief should push back on, not accept at face value.
5. Identify unresolved open questions the panel raised (things no one asked, or an answer that came back vague) and list them as explicit items - if there's still time, these become questions for a reference check or a short follow-up call rather than being silently dropped.
6. End with a proposed framing for the decision (not the decision itself) - e.g. "panel is aligned on X, split on Y, recommend focusing debrief time on resolving Y" - so the meeting starts with the real tension instead of restating everyone's score.

## Output format

```
# Debrief Summary - [Candidate Name] for [Role]

## Competency coverage grid
| Competency | Interviewer(s) who assessed | Ratings | Assessed at all? |
|---|---|---|---|
| ... | ... | ... | Y/N |

## Where the panel agrees
- [Competency]: all interviewers rated [X], evidence: [specific quote/example]

## Where the panel disagrees
- [Competency]: [Interviewer A] rated [X] ("[evidence]") vs. [Interviewer B] rated [Y] ("[evidence]") - needs discussion

## Thin evidence flags
- [Interviewer]'s rating of [X] on [competency] has no specific supporting example - worth probing in debrief

## Open questions / gaps
- [Competency or concern never actually tested]

## Suggested debrief focus
[1-2 sentences on what the meeting should actually resolve]
```

## Do
- Surface disagreement explicitly instead of collapsing it into an average
- Quote specific evidence from scorecards rather than paraphrasing into vague praise
- Flag competencies that no interviewer actually tested

## Don't
- Don't present a single averaged score as if consensus existed when it didn't
- Don't make the hiring recommendation yourself - this summary sets up the meeting, the panel decides
- Don't smooth over a rating with thin evidence just because it was positive

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disagreements are shown explicitly, not averaged away
- [ ] Competency coverage grid reveals any untested must-have areas
- [ ] Summary avoids making the final hiring decision itself
```

# /job-description-writer

Produces a ready-to-post job description with clearly separated must-have vs. nice-to-have requirements, a realistic day-in-the-life summary, and language checked for exclusionary bias.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A hiring manager or recruiter has an open headcount approved and needs a job posting written from scratch, or an existing JD is stale (wrong title, outdated stack, requirements that don't match how the team actually works) and needs a rewrite before it goes back on job boards.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Role title, team/department, and who it reports to
- Top 3-5 outcomes this person needs to deliver in the first year (not just tasks)
- Required skills/experience vs. skills that are genuinely optional but a plus
- Compensation range and key benefits (if the company wants them listed - some don't, confirm before assuming)
- Location/remote policy and any visa/work-authorization constraints
- Team size and reporting structure, so "day in the life" reads accurately

## Workflow

1. Separate true dealbreakers from wish-list items. Ask: "would we reject a candidate who is missing this but strong everywhere else?" If no, it goes in nice-to-have, not required - inflated requirement lists are the single biggest cause of good candidates self-selecting out.
2. Write responsibilities as outcomes ("own the checkout funnel's conversion rate") rather than task lists ("write code"), so the JD differentiates this role from a generic one with the same title elsewhere.
3. Run a bias pass on the draft: strip gendered coding words (e.g. "ninja," "rockstar," "aggressive"), remove unnecessary degree requirements if the work doesn't need them, and check requirement counts - research on application drop-off suggests postings with more than ~7 required items suppress applications from qualified women and underrepresented candidates disproportionately, so trim to the real essentials.
4. Add a concrete "what success looks like at 90 days / 1 year" line - this does more to attract the right candidates than another bullet of buzzwords.
5. Decide on pay transparency: if the user gave a range, include it in the format their target job boards expect (many US states now require it); if no range was given, flag this as a gap rather than inventing one.
6. Assemble the final posting in the output format below and do a final read for tone consistency (should sound like one voice, not a stitched list).

## Output format

```
# [Job Title] - [Department]

**Location:** [city/remote policy]  |  **Reports to:** [role]  |  **Type:** [FT/PT/contract]

## About the role
[2-3 sentences: why this role exists now, what changes because it's filled]

## What you'll own
- [Outcome 1]
- [Outcome 2]
- [Outcome 3]

## What success looks like
- 90 days: [milestone]
- 12 months: [milestone]

## Must-have
- [item]
- [item]

## Nice-to-have
- [item]
- [item]

## Compensation & benefits
[Range if provided; benefits list; note if range is a placeholder pending user input]

## How to apply
[Instructions placeholder]
```

## Do
- Cap "must-have" at 5-6 real dealbreakers
- Use active, specific verbs tied to outcomes, not personality adjectives
- Flag missing comp range explicitly rather than guessing a number

## Don't
- Don't invent a salary range if none was given - leave a clear placeholder
- Don't pad requirements to sound impressive; it shrinks the applicant pool
- Don't copy generic corporate boilerplate language that could describe any company

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Must-have list contains only true dealbreakers, not wish-list items
- [ ] Bias-language pass completed (no gendered coding, no unnecessary degree gate)
- [ ] Comp/benefits section either filled from user input or clearly flagged as missing
```

# /offer-letter-draft

Drafts a candidate-facing offer letter covering compensation, start date, and standard contingencies, plus a separate internal talking-points sheet for the hiring manager to use on the verbal offer call.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A candidate has cleared all interview stages, references are done, and the hiring manager or recruiter needs both the written offer letter and a script for the verbal call that typically happens before the written offer is sent, so the numbers and tone match.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Candidate name, title, manager, start date, and work location/remote status
- Base compensation, and any bonus/equity/commission structure
- Standard contingencies that apply (background check, reference check, right-to-work/visa verification, at-will language per company policy)
- Key benefits or perks the company wants highlighted (PTO policy, health coverage start date, signing bonus if any)
- Anything the candidate specifically asked about or negotiated during the process, so the verbal call addresses it directly

## Safety

- This is a draft for the user's own review and their legal/HR team's sign-off - it is not a final legal employment contract. Advise the user to have qualified legal or HR counsel confirm the letter meets local employment law (at-will language, required disclosures, contingency wording) before it is sent.
- Propose before acting: show the full draft and talking points; wait for explicit user confirmation before anything is sent to the candidate or presented on a call.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (sending the offer, confirming compensation verbally) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Draft the written offer letter first, in formal but warm tone, with every number and date explicit - no placeholders left in a document meant to go to a candidate.
2. List every contingency clearly and separately (background check, references, employment eligibility verification) so the candidate understands the offer isn't fully final until each clears.
3. Build the internal talking-points sheet as a call script, not a copy of the letter: opening excitement line, the numbers spoken out loud in plain language, and anticipated candidate questions with suggested answers (e.g. "if they ask about remote flexibility, say X").
4. Flag any point where the offer differs from what the candidate discussed or negotiated during interviews, so the hiring manager can address the gap proactively on the call rather than being caught off guard.
5. Add a "what NOT to promise verbally" note to the talking points - anything not in the written letter (schedule flexibility, promotion timelines, specific project assignments) should be flagged so the hiring manager doesn't verbally commit to something HR/legal hasn't approved.
6. Include a clear response deadline and next-steps line in both documents so the candidate knows exactly what happens after they say yes.

## Output format

```
## Candidate-facing offer letter

[Date]
Dear [Candidate Name],

[Formal offer body: title, manager, start date, location, compensation breakdown, benefits summary, contingencies, response deadline, signature block]

---
## Internal: verbal offer call talking points (not for candidate)

Opening line: [warm, specific opener]
Numbers to state out loud: [plain-language walkthrough]
Anticipated questions & suggested responses:
  - Q: [likely question] → A: [suggested response]
Gaps vs. what candidate discussed earlier: [flag any]
Do NOT verbally promise: [list]
Next steps to state on the call: [deadline, what happens after acceptance]
```

## Do
- Spell out every number, date, and contingency explicitly with no placeholders in the candidate-facing letter
- Flag any gap between what's being offered and what the candidate previously discussed
- Keep the internal talking points clearly separated and marked "not for candidate"

## Don't
- Don't let the hiring manager's talking points include unapproved verbal promises (promotion timing, project guarantees)
- Don't send or "finalize" anything without the user's explicit go-ahead - this is a draft pending their review
- Don't omit contingencies (background check, right-to-work) even if the user didn't mention them, if they're standard for the company/location

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present that this is a draft pending legal/HR review, not a final legal document
- [ ] Approval gate present before letter is sent or verbal offer is made
- [ ] Every compensation figure and date is explicit - no unresolved placeholders in candidate-facing text
```

# /reference-check-questions

Builds a reference-check call guide targeted at the specific risk areas or open questions this candidate's interviews raised, with a structured log format so answers from multiple references can be compared side by side.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A candidate is a finalist and the hiring team wants to verify claims and probe specific concerns before extending an offer - most useful when interviews surfaced a soft spot (e.g. "we're not fully sure how they handle conflict" or "their story about leaving their last role was vague") that a generic reference-check script won't catch.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Role and what this hire needs to be great at in the first 90 days
- Any specific concern or open question from the interview loop that references could help resolve
- The reference's relationship to the candidate (direct manager, peer, direct report, client) since the right questions differ by relationship
- Whether the candidate's current employer knows they're job hunting (affects whether you can call their current manager)

## Safety

- Reference checks must comply with applicable employment law - some jurisdictions restrict what can be asked or shared (e.g. only "confirm employment dates" policies at some companies). Advise the user to confirm their reference-check practice is compliant with local law and their own company's policy before calling.
- Propose before acting: show the question guide; wait for explicit user confirmation before any reference calls are made.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action (calling a reference, especially a current employer) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Start every guide with 2-3 confirmation questions (title, dates, reporting relationship) - this establishes baseline honesty before moving to open-ended questions, and mismatches here are themselves a signal worth flagging.
2. Write 1-2 questions that directly target the specific concern from the interview loop, phrased to elicit a story, not a yes/no (e.g. if the concern was "handles conflict," ask "tell me about a time they disagreed with a decision from above - what did they do").
3. Include a "would you rehire them" or "would you want to work with them again" question - the hesitation (or lack of it) in how this is answered is often more informative than the words used.
4. Add one question calibrated to the reference's specific relationship (a former manager gets asked about growth trajectory and areas to develop; a peer gets asked about collaboration; a direct report gets asked about how the candidate gave feedback and support).
5. Build a structured log format for the call, not just an empty notes box, so multiple reference calls can be compared for consistent patterns vs. one-off impressions.
6. End with a note reminding the caller to listen for hesitation, vague non-answers, or "no comment" on standard questions - often more telling than what's explicitly said, but should be recorded as an observation, not over-interpreted as fact.

## Output format

```
# Reference Check Guide - [Candidate Name] for [Role]
Reference: [Name, relationship to candidate, contact info]

## Baseline confirmation
- Confirm title and dates of employment: ___________
- Confirm reporting relationship: ___________

## Targeted questions (based on interview concern: [state concern])
Q: [question]
  Notes: ___________
Q: [question]
  Notes: ___________

## Relationship-specific question ([manager/peer/report/client])
Q: [question]
  Notes: ___________

## Closing signal question
Q: "Would you [rehire them / want to work with them again]?"
  Notes: ___________
  Hesitation or tone observed: ___________

## Overall impression
Consistent with interview signals? Y/N - notes: ___________
```

## Do
- Target at least one question directly at the specific concern raised in interviews
- Log answers in a structured, comparable format across multiple references
- Note tone and hesitation as observations, separate from the literal answer

## Don't
- Don't call a candidate's current employer without confirming the candidate has authorized it
- Don't ask questions unrelated to job performance (personal life, health, family status)
- Don't treat one reference's opinion as final - look for patterns across all references called

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming compliance with employment law/company policy
- [ ] Approval gate present before any reference call is made
- [ ] At least one question directly targets the specific concern the user described
```

# /rejection-email-templates

Writes a candidate rejection email calibrated to the exact stage they were rejected at, with an optional constructive-feedback version for finalists who invested significant time.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A recruiter needs to close out a candidate who is not moving forward - whether that's an auto-decline after resume screen, a decline after a phone screen, or the harder message to a finalist who made it through multiple rounds and deserves more than a form letter.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which stage the candidate was rejected at (application-only, phone screen, one or more interviews, final-round/finalist)
- Whether the company wants to offer specific feedback (only appropriate for later stages, and only if legal/HR is comfortable with it - some companies have a no-feedback policy to avoid disputes)
- Whether the role might be a fit for something else later (keep-warm vs. full close)
- Company name and any specific positive note from the interview worth including for finalists

## Safety

- This is general drafting help, not legal advice - feedback language in rejection emails can carry legal risk (e.g. comments that could be read as touching protected characteristics, age, disability, family status). Advise the user to confirm any feedback language with their legal/HR function before sending, especially for regulated jurisdictions.
- Propose before acting: show the draft; wait for explicit user confirmation before it is sent to any candidate.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Match the message weight to the stage: an application-only reject can be a brief, warm form email; a post-onsite-interview reject to a finalist needs personal acknowledgment of their time and effort, not a template.
2. Never state a false or vague reason ("we went with someone whose experience more closely aligned") if it isn't true - vague language is fine, but avoid specifics that could later look pretextual or discriminatory if challenged.
3. If feedback was requested and approved, keep it behavior-based and job-relevant only (e.g. "the team was looking for deeper hands-on experience with X system") - never comment on personality, communication style in ways that could imply bias, or anything unrelated to demonstrated job-relevant skills.
4. For finalists, name one genuine positive from their process - it costs nothing and materially changes how the rejection lands, especially since finalists often become future candidates, referrers, or even customers.
5. Keep a "future fit" option open only if genuinely true - inviting someone to reapply when the company has no intention of considering them again damages trust and the employer brand.
6. Close with a clear, unambiguous ending - no vague "we'll keep your resume on file" unless the company actually does that.

## Output format

```
## Rejection email - Stage: [application / phone screen / onsite / finalist]

Subject: [subject line]

[Email body: opening acknowledgment, decision statement, optional feedback,
optional future-fit note, warm closing]

---
[If finalist and feedback approved:]
## Optional feedback add-on (only send if legal/HR has approved sharing feedback)
[Behavior-based, job-relevant feedback only]
```

## Do
- Match message length and warmth to how much time the candidate invested
- Keep any feedback strictly behavior-based and job-relevant
- Name a genuine positive for finalists

## Don't
- Don't give specific feedback unless the user has confirmed it's approved by legal/HR
- Don't invite someone to "reapply in future" unless that's genuinely true
- Don't send a template-feeling form email to someone who did multiple onsite rounds

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present noting feedback language should be confirmed with legal/HR before sending
- [ ] Approval gate present before any email is sent
- [ ] Message weight matches the actual stage the candidate reached
```

A recruiter needs to close out a candidate who is not moving forward - whether that's an auto-decline after resume screen, a decline after a phone screen, or the harder message to a finalist who made it through multiple rounds and deserves more than a form letter.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which stage the candidate was rejected at (application-only, phone screen, one or more interviews, final-round/finalist)
- Whether the company wants to offer specific feedback (only appropriate for later stages, and only if legal/HR is comfortable with it - some companies have a no-feedback policy to avoid disputes)
- Whether the role might be a fit for something else later (keep-warm vs. full close)
- Company name and any specific positive note from the interview worth including for finalists

## Safety

- This is general drafting help, not legal advice - feedback language in rejection emails can carry legal risk (e.g. comments that could be read as touching protected characteristics, age, disability, family status). Advise the user to confirm any feedback language with their legal/HR function before sending, especially for regulated jurisdictions.
- Propose before acting: show the draft; wait for explicit user confirmation before it is sent to any candidate.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Match the message weight to the stage: an application-only reject can be a brief, warm form email; a post-onsite-interview reject to a finalist needs personal acknowledgment of their time and effort, not a template.
2. Never state a false or vague reason ("we went with someone whose experience more closely aligned") if it isn't true - vague language is fine, but avoid specifics that could later look pretextual or discriminatory if challenged.
3. If feedback was requested and approved, keep it behavior-based and job-relevant only (e.g. "the team was looking for deeper hands-on experience with X system") - never comment on personality, communication style in ways that could imply bias, or anything unrelated to demonstrated job-relevant skills.
4. For finalists, name one genuine positive from their process - it costs nothing and materially changes how the rejection lands, especially since finalists often become future candidates, referrers, or even customers.
5. Keep a "future fit" option open only if genuinely true - inviting someone to reapply when the company has no intention of considering them again damages trust and the employer brand.
6. Close with a clear, unambiguous ending - no vague "we'll keep your resume on file" unless the company actually does that.

## Output format

```
## Rejection email - Stage: [application / phone screen / onsite / finalist]

Subject: [subject line]

[Email body: opening acknowledgment, decision statement, optional feedback,
optional future-fit note, warm closing]

---
[If finalist and feedback approved:]
## Optional feedback add-on (only send if legal/HR has approved sharing feedback)
[Behavior-based, job-relevant feedback only]
```

## Do
- Match message length and warmth to how much time the candidate invested
- Keep any feedback strictly behavior-based and job-relevant
- Name a genuine positive for finalists

## Don't
- Don't give specific feedback unless the user has confirmed it's approved by legal/HR
- Don't invite someone to "reapply in future" unless that's genuinely true
- Don't send a template-feeling form email to someone who did multiple onsite rounds

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present noting feedback language should be confirmed with legal/HR before sending
- [ ] Approval gate present before any email is sent
- [ ] Message weight matches the actual stage the candidate reached

# /structured-interview-scorecard

Builds a competency-based interview scorecard: 3-5 role-critical competencies, 2 behavioral questions per competency, and a 1-4 rating rubric with anchor descriptions so different interviewers score consistently.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A hiring team is about to start interview loops and either has no scorecard (interviewers will wing it and everyone will ask the same two questions) or has an old scorecard with vague ratings like "good culture fit" that don't hold up if a rejected candidate ever challenges the decision.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Role and level (the same title can need very different competencies at junior vs. senior)
- The job description or must-have list, if one already exists from job-description-writer or elsewhere
- Which interview stage this scorecard is for (recruiter screen, hiring manager, panel, technical, final)
- Any competency the team already knows is critical from past mis-hires (e.g. "we've had people fail at stakeholder pushback before")

## Safety

- This scorecard is a structuring tool for the hiring team's own judgment, not a legal compliance document - advise the user to have their HR/legal function confirm it aligns with local employment law before it becomes an official company template.
- Propose before acting: show the draft scorecard; wait for explicit user confirmation before it is distributed to interviewers or used to make a hiring decision.
- Never take an irreversible or external-facing action without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Pull 3-5 competencies directly from the role's actual outcomes, not a generic list - e.g. a support-team lead needs "de-escalation under pressure," not a boilerplate "communication skills" entry with no teeth.
2. For each competency, write exactly two behavioral (past-experience) questions using a "tell me about a time" or "walk me through" structure, phrased so they can't be answered hypothetically - this is what makes answers checkable against real evidence instead of rehearsed opinions.
3. Write a 4-point rating anchor for every competency (1 = no evidence/red flag, 2 = below bar, 3 = meets bar, 4 = exceeds bar) with one concrete behavioral example at each level specific to that competency, not a generic 1-4 scale reused everywhere.
4. Add a "evidence" field under each question, not just a score box - interviewers must write what the candidate actually said, so ratings are defensible later and not just gut feel.
5. Include one probing follow-up per question to catch rehearsed or vague answers (e.g. "what would you do differently next time?").
6. Add a final "overall recommendation" section that forces a strong-no/no/yes/strong-yes plus one sentence of reasoning, separate from the average of competency scores, so interviewers can't just average their way to a false consensus.

## Output format

```
# Interview Scorecard - [Role] ([Level]) - [Stage]

## Competency 1: [Name]
Why it matters for this role: [one line]

Q1: [behavioral question]
  Follow-up: [probe]
  Evidence: ___________
  Rating: 1 2 3 4
    1 = [anchor] | 2 = [anchor] | 3 = [anchor] | 4 = [anchor]

Q2: [behavioral question]
  ...(same structure)

[repeat per competency]

## Overall recommendation
Recommendation: Strong No / No / Yes / Strong Yes
Reasoning (1-2 sentences): ___________
Any concerns to flag to the next interviewer: ___________
```

## Do
- Tie every competency to a real, named outcome from the job, not a personality trait
- Force written evidence, not just a number, for every question
- Keep the overall recommendation separate from the math average of scores

## Don't
- Don't include vague competencies like "culture fit" without defining observable behaviors
- Don't write hypothetical ("what would you do if...") questions as the primary format - they invite rehearsed answers
- Don't let the scorecard exceed 5 competencies; more than that and interviewers rush and rate on vibes

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present before scorecard is distributed or used to decide
- [ ] Every competency has a distinct, concrete rating anchor at all 4 levels
- [ ] Questions are behavioral (past-tense, evidence-based), not hypothetical
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /structured-interview-scorecard or describe the task from the skill description.
